blocks:
Ayahs

تَبَـٰرَكَ ٱلَّذِى بِيَدِهِ ٱلْمُلْكُ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١)
Blessed is He in whose hands is all sovereignty, and He has power over all things. (1)
ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلْمَوْتَ وَٱلْحَيَوٰةَ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْغَفُورُ (٢)
He, who created death and life that He may test you [to see] which of you is best in conduct. And He is the All-mighty, the All-forgiving. (2)
ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَٰتٍ طِبَاقًا ۖ مَّا تَرَىٰ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ مِن تَفَـٰوُتٍ ۖ فَٱرْجِعِ ٱلْبَصَرَ هَلْ تَرَىٰ مِن فُطُورٍ (٣)
He created seven heavens in layers. You do not see any discordance in the creation of the All-beneficent. Look again! Do you see any flaw? (3)
ثُمَّ ٱرْجِعِ ٱلْبَصَرَ كَرَّتَيْنِ يَنقَلِبْ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْبَصَرُ خَاسِئًا وَهُوَ حَسِيرٌ (٤)
Look again, once more. Your look will return to you humbled and weary. (4)
وَلَقَدْ زَيَّنَّا ٱلسَّمَآءَ ٱلدُّنْيَا بِمَصَـٰبِيحَ وَجَعَلْنَـٰهَا رُجُومًا لِّلشَّيَـٰطِينِ ۖ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لَهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (٥)
We have certainly adorned the lowest heaven with lamps, and made them [the means of pelting] missiles against the devils, and We have prepared for them a punishment of the Blaze. (5)
وَلِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِرَبِّهِمْ عَذَابُ جَهَنَّمَ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٦)
For those who defy their Lord is the punishment of hell, and it is an evil destination. (6)
إِذَآ أُلْقُوا۟ فِيهَا سَمِعُوا۟ لَهَا شَهِيقًا وَهِىَ تَفُورُ (٧)
When they are thrown in it, they hear it blaring, as it seethes, (7)
تَكَادُ تَمَيَّزُ مِنَ ٱلْغَيْظِ ۖ كُلَّمَآ أُلْقِىَ فِيهَا فَوْجٌ سَأَلَهُمْ خَزَنَتُهَآ أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَذِيرٌ (٨)
almost exploding with rage. Whenever a group is thrown in it, its keepers will ask them, ‘Did not any warner come to you?’ (8)
قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ قَدْ جَآءَنَا نَذِيرٌ فَكَذَّبْنَا وَقُلْنَا مَا نَزَّلَ ٱللَّهُ مِن شَىْءٍ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ كَبِيرٍ (٩)
They will say, ‘Yes, a warner did come to us, but we impugned [him] and said, ‘Allah did not send down anything; you are only in great error.’ (9)
وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ مَا كُنَّا فِىٓ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (١٠)
And they will say, ‘Had we listened or applied reason, we would not have been among inmates of the Blaze.’ (10)
فَٱعْتَرَفُوا۟ بِذَنۢبِهِمْ فَسُحْقًا لِّأَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (١١)
Thus they will admit their sin. So away with the inmates of the Blaze! (11)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَيْبِ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ (١٢)
Indeed for those who fear their Lord in secret there will be forgiveness and a great reward. (12)
وَأَسِرُّوا۟ قَوْلَكُمْ أَوِ ٱجْهَرُوا۟ بِهِۦٓ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (١٣)
Speak secretly, or do so openly, indeed He knows well what is in the breasts. (13)
أَلَا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ خَلَقَ وَهُوَ ٱللَّطِيفُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ (١٤)
Would He who has created not know? And He is the All-attentive, the All-aware. (14)

(Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is to explain the universality of His Lordship (الربوبية) — the absolute sovereignty and sustaining governance of Allah over all creation — over all the worlds, in response to the claims of the idolaters who asserted that every part of the world has a lord from among the angels and others, and that He, the Exalted, is only the Lord of lords.

For this reason, He, the Glorified, enumerates many of His blessings in creation (الخلق) and governance (التدبير) — which serves as an argumentation for His Lordship — and opens the discourse with His being blessed (تباركه), which refers to the abundance of blessings (البركات) emanating from Him. He repeatedly describes Himself as the All-Beneficent (الرحمن) — the possessor of expansive mercy that encompasses all things — which is an intensive form of mercy (الرحمة) that signifies giving in response to the call of poverty. Within this is a warning that culminates in the mention of the Gathering (الحشر) and the Resurrection (البعث).

The contents of its verses can be summarized as a call to the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) and the affirmation of the Return (المعاد) — the ultimate return of all beings to Allah for judgment.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context (سياق) of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: تَبارَكَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ الْمُلْكُ وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ — Blessed is He in whose hand is the kingdom, and He has power over all things. (1)

The term تبارك (Blessed) regarding a thing means the abundance of goodness and blessings emanating from it.

His saying: الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ الْمُلْكُ (in whose hand is the kingdom) includes, by its absolute nature (إطلاقه), every form of sovereignty. Placing the kingdom "in His hand" is a metaphorical expression (استعارة بالكناية) for the perfection of His dominance over it and His disposal (تصرف) of it as He wills, just as a possessor of a hand disposes of what is in his hand and turns it as he wishes. Thus, He, the Exalted, possesses by His very essence everything from all its aspects, and He possesses whatever is possessed by anything else.

Therefore, describing Him, the Exalted, as the one "in whose hand is the kingdom" is broader than describing Him as the "Sovereign" (المليك) in His saying: عِنْدَ مَلِيكٍ مُقْتَدِرٍ — in the presence of a puissant King (54:55), and it is more explicit and emphatic than His description in: لَهُ الْمُلْكُ — to Him belongs the kingdom (64:1).

His saying: وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (and He has power over all things) is an indication that His power (قدرته) is not limited by any boundary nor does it end at any limit. This is a necessary consequence of the absolute nature of the kingdom according to the context, even though the absolute nature of the kingdom — which is an attribute of action (صفات الفعل) — is among the necessary consequences of absolute power, which is an attribute of the essence (صفات الذات).

Furthermore, the verse contains an allusion to the proof for the possibility of the matter of the Return (المعاد) that is to follow.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْمَوْتَ وَالْحَياةَ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفُورُ — He, who created death and life that He may test you [to see] which of you is best in conduct. And He is the All-mighty, the All-forgiving. (2)

Life (الحياة) is for a thing to be such that it perceives and wills, and death (الموت) is the absence of that. However, death, according to what appears from the teachings of the Quran, is a transition from one stage (نشأة) of the stages of life to another stage, as previously derived from His saying, the Exalted: نَحْنُ قَدَّرْنا بَيْنَكُمُ الْمَوْتَ (We have ordained death among you...) until His saying: فِي ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ (...in that which you do not know) (56:60-61). Thus, there is no obstacle to creation (الخلق) being associated with death just as it is with life.

Moreover, even if death were taken as a privative concept (عدمياً) as per common convention (العرف), it would be the privation of the faculty of life (عدم ملكة الحياة), and it possesses a share of existence (حظ من الوجود) that justifies the association of creation with it, similar to blindness in relation to sight and darkness in relation to light.

His saying: لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً (that He may test you [to see] which of you is best in conduct) is the purpose (غاية) of His creation of death and life. Testing (البلاء) means examination (الامتحان). The meaning is that this type of creation — which is that you live and then die — is a preliminary, examinational creation by which those among you who are better in conduct are distinguished from others. It is well known that examination and distinction only occur for a matter that awaits you after this, which is the recompense (جزاء) of each according to their deed.

The discourse also contains an indication that the primary intended purpose (المقصود بالذات) of creation is the delivery of the good of the recompense, as He mentioned the excellence of conduct and the distinction of those who bring the best of it. Thus, those who are best in conduct are the intended ones of creation, and others are intended for their sake.

He concluded the discourse with His saying: وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفُورُ (And He is the All-mighty, the All-forgiving). He is the All-mighty (العزيز) because absolute kingdom and power belong to Him alone; no overcomer can overcome Him, and He has not empowered anyone to disobey Him except as a trial and examination, and He shall take vengeance upon them. He is the All-forgiving (الغفور) because He pardons many of their evil deeds in this world and will forgive many of them in the Hereafter as He has promised.

In concluding with these two names, there is also an element of instilling fear and hope (تخويف وتطميع) as required by the context of the call.

Know that the content of the verse is not merely a claim devoid of proof intended for indoctrination (تلقين), as might be imagined. Rather, it is a premise close to necessity (الضرورة) — or it is necessary — that demands the judgment of the necessity of the Resurrection for recompense. For a human being clothed in this worldly life followed by death is not free from acquiring the description of "best in conduct" or its opposite. He is equipped, according to the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — with that which, were it not for the occurrence of an evil accident, would lead him to good conduct. Rarely is a human being free from acquiring one of the two descriptions, such as children and those in their position.

The acquired description resulting from the existence of a thing, which pervades most of its individuals, is a purpose (غاية) in its existence and intended in its creation. Just as the vegetative life of a certain tree, since it mostly leads to the production of a certain fruit, is considered the purpose of its existence and intended from it, likewise, righteousness and good conduct are the purpose of the creation of man. It is also known that righteousness and good conduct, if desired, are desired for something else, not for themselves. What is desired for its own sake is the "wholesome life" (الحياة الطيبة) that is not tainted by deficiency nor visited by vain talk or sinfulness. Thus, the verse is similar in meaning to His saying: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذائِقَةُ الْمَوْتِ وَنَبْلُوكُمْ بِالشَّرِّ وَالْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً — Every soul shall taste death, and We will test you with good and ill by way of a trial (21:35).

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ طِباقاً — He, who created seven heavens in layers. (3) etc. That is, matching one above the other, or some resembling others — as has been conjectured. Some of what we can say regarding this has already passed in the commentary of Surah Ha Mim al-Sajdah.

His saying: ما تَرى فِي خَلْقِ الرَّحْمنِ مِنْ تَفاوُتٍ — You do not see any discordance in the creation of the All-beneficent. (3). Al-Raghib said: Fawt (الفوت) is the distance of a thing from a person such that its perception becomes impossible. The Exalted said: وَإِنْ فاتَكُمْ شَيْءٌ مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ إِلَى الْكُفَّارِ — And if any of your wives should go over to the faithless (60:11).

He said: Tafawut (التفاوت) is the difference in attributes, as if the attribute of one misses (يفوت) the other, or the attribute of each misses the other. The Exalted said: ما تَرى فِي خَلْقِ الرَّحْمنِ مِنْ تَفاوُتٍ meaning there is nothing in it that departs from the requirements of wisdom (الحكمة). [End of quote].

The intended meaning of denying discordance (التفاوت) is the continuity of governance (التدبير) and the interconnectedness of things with one another in terms of the ends (الغايات) and benefits resulting from their mutual interaction. The clashing of different causes in creation and their contention is like the struggle between the two pans of a scale, contending through weight and lightness, rising and falling; for while they differ, they agree in assisting the one who holds the scale in what he intends of determining the weight of the weighed commodity.

Thus, Allah has arranged the parts of creation such that they lead to their objectives without any part missing the purpose of another, or any part missing the attribute necessary for it to achieve the desired end.

The address in ما تَرى (You do not see) is a general address to everyone capable of seeing. In attributing creation to the All-Beneficent (الرحمن), there is an indication that the purpose of it is general mercy. The indefiniteness (تنكير) of تَفاوُتٍ (discordance) in the context of negation, and the introduction of the name "the All-Beneficent" over it, is to signify universality (العموم).

His saying: فَارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ هَلْ تَرى مِنْ فُطُورٍ — Look again! Do you see any flaw? (3). Futor (الفطور) is disorder and weakness. The meaning of "returning the sight" (إرجاع البصر) is to look a second time, which is a metaphor for precision in looking and deep contemplation.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ ارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ كَرَّتَيْنِ يَنْقَلِبْ إِلَيْكَ الْبَصَرُ خاسِئاً وَهُوَ حَسِيرٌ — Then look again and again: your look will return to you humbled and weary. (4). Al-Khasi' (الخاسئ) is from the word for the eye being "driven back" (خسأ) when it withdraws out of humiliation, as Al-Raghib said. He also said: Al-Hasir (الحاسر) is the exhausted one due to the depletion of his powers. The exhausted one is called hasir and mahsūr. As for hasir, it is conceived that he himself has exhausted his power, and as for mahsūr, it is conceived that fatigue has exhausted him. The saying of the Mighty and Majestic: يَنْقَلِبْ إِلَيْكَ الْبَصَرُ خاسِئاً وَهُوَ حَسِيرٌ can correctly mean hasir or mahsūr. [End of quote].

His saying: كَرَّتَيْنِ (twice/again and again). Al-Karrah (الكرة) is the return, and the dual form here is intended to mean multiplicity and repetition. The meaning is: then return the sight return after return — i.e., many returns — and the sight will return to you withdrawn and humbled, while it is dull and exhausted, having found no flaw.

Thus, it is indicated in these two verses that the system running in the universe is a single system, continuous in its parts and interconnected in its dimensions.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ زَيَّنَّا السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا بِمَصابِيحَ — We have certainly adorned the lowest heaven with lamps (5) until the end of the verse. Masabih (المصابيح) is the plural of misbah, which is a lamp. The stars are called lamps because of their illumination and brightness. Discussion on this has already passed in the commentary of Surah Ha Mim al-Sajdah.

His saying: وَجَعَلْناها رُجُوماً لِلشَّياطِينِ — and made them missiles for the devils (5). That is, We made the stars with which We adorned the heaven as missiles with which the devils who eavesdrop are pelted, as the Exalted said: إِلَّا مَنِ اسْتَرَقَ السَّمْعَ فَأَتْبَعَهُ شِهابٌ مُبِينٌ — except him who eavesdrops, whereat a manifest flame pursues him (15:18), and He said: إِلَّا مَنْ خَطِفَ الْخَطْفَةَ فَأَتْبَعَهُ شِهابٌ ثاقِبٌ — except him who snatches a fragment, whereat a piercing flame pursues him (37:10).

It is said: The sentence is evidence that what is meant by the stars with which the heaven is adorned is the totality of the original stars and the celestial meteors (الشهب), for the original stars do not move from their fixed positions. The terms "star" (الكوكب) and "heavenly body" (النجم) are applied to meteors just as they are applied to the original celestial bodies.

It is also said: Meteors separate from the stars to become missiles for the devils, while the stars themselves do not move unless Allah intends their annihilation.

This view is more in line with contemporary scientific perspectives. Some discussion has already passed regarding the meaning of pelting the devils with meteors.

His saying: وَأَعْتَدْنا لَهُمْ عَذابَ السَّعِيرِ — and We have prepared for them the punishment of the Blaze. (5). That is, We have prepared for the devils — who are the wicked among the Jinn — the punishment of the kindled, flaming fire.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ عَذابُ جَهَنَّمَ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ — For those who defy their Lord is the punishment of hell, and it is an evil destination. (6). When He presented some of the signs of His Lordship, He followed them with the threat against those who disbelieve in His Lordship, as is the characteristic of this Surah in the interweaving of proofs, threats, and warnings.

What is meant by "those who disbelieve in their Lord" is broader than the idolaters who deny His Lordship for any other than their "lords" and say that He, the Exalted, is only the Lord of lords; and those who deny it absolutely; and those who affirm His Lordship while differentiating between Him and His messengers, like the Jews and Christians who believed in some of His messengers and disbelieved in others.

The verse is also connected to His saying: الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْمَوْتَ وَالْحَياةَ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفُورُ because of the indication it contains regarding the Resurrection and recompense, and it is connected to what preceded it as a generalization after a specification.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذا أُلْقُوا فِيها سَمِعُوا لَها شَهِيقاً وَهِيَ تَفُورُ تَكادُ تَمَيَّزُ مِنَ الْغَيْظِ — When they are thrown into it, they hear it gasping as it seethes. It almost bursts with fury. (7-8). Al-Raghib said: Shahīq (الشهيق) is the length of the inhalation (الزفير), which is the drawing in of breath, and Zafīr (الزفير) is its extension. [End of quote]. Fawaran (الفوران), as in al-Majma', is the rising of boiling. Tamayyuz (التميز) is to break apart and separate. Ghayz (الغيظ) is the intensity of anger. The meaning is: when the disbelievers are cast into Hell, they hear it gasping — that is, it draws them into its interior just as air is drawn by inhalation into the chest — while it boils with them, raising and lowering them, almost disintegrating from the intensity of its anger.

His saying, the Exalted: كُلَّما أُلْقِيَ فِيها فَوْجٌ سَأَلَهُمْ خَزَنَتُها أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَذِيرٌ — Whenever a group is thrown into it, its keepers will ask them, ‘Did there not come to you any warner?’ (8). Fawj (الفوج) — as Al-Raghib said — is a group passing quickly. In His saying: كُلَّما أُلْقِيَ فِيها فَوْجٌ there is an indication that the disbelievers are cast into the Fire group by group, as indicated by His saying: وَسِيقَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلى جَهَنَّمَ زُمَراً — The faithless will be driven to hell in throngs (39:71). They are cast in this manner because of the followers joining their leaders in misguidance, as the Exalted said: وَيَجْعَلَ الْخَبِيثَ بَعْضَهُ عَلى بَعْضٍ فَيَرْكُمَهُ جَمِيعاً فَيَجْعَلَهُ فِي جَهَنَّمَ — and put the bad ones one on top of another, and heap them all together and put them into hell (8:37). Some clarification of this has already passed under that verse in Surah al-Anfal.

Khazanah (الخزنة) is the plural of khāzin, who is the guardian over a stored thing. What is meant by them are the angels entrusted with the Fire, who manage the types of its punishment. The Exalted said: عَلَيْها مَلائِكَةٌ غِلاظٌ شِدادٌ — over it are angels, stern and severe (66:6), and He said: وَما أَدْراكَ ما سَقَرُ (And what will show you what is Saqar?) until He said: عَلَيْها تِسْعَةَ عَشَرَ وَما جَعَلْنا أَصْحابَ النَّارِ إِلَّا مَلائِكَةً — Over it are nineteen. We have appointed only angels as keepers of the Fire (74:27-31).

The meaning is: whenever a group from the groups of disbelievers driven to Hell is cast into it, the angels entrusted with the Fire and guarding it ask them — by way of rebuke — "Did there not come to you a warner?" meaning the warning Prophet.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا بَلى قَدْ جاءَنا نَذِيرٌ فَكَذَّبْنا — They will say, ‘Yes, a warner did come to us, but we impugned [him] (9) until the end of the verse. This is a narration of their answer to the question of the keepers, and in it is a confirmation that a warner did indeed come to them, whom they then attributed to falsehood, and an admission.

His saying: ما نَزَّلَ اللهُ مِنْ شَيْءٍ (Allah has not sent down anything) is an explanation of their denial, as is His saying: إِنْ أَنْتُمْ إِلَّا فِي ضَلالٍ كَبِيرٍ (You are only in great error). It is said: His saying: إِنْ أَنْتُمْ etc., is the speech of the angels addressing the disbelievers after their answer to the question; however, this is far-fetched from the context. Likewise is the possibility of it being from the speech of the messengers whom they denied, narrated by the angels to those disbelievers.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ ما كُنَّا فِي أَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ — And they will say, ‘Had we listened or applied reason, we would not have been among the inmates of the Blaze.’ (10). Hearing (السمع) is used to mean the perception of sound and speech by the organ, and sometimes it is used to mean its ultimate purpose among the rational, which is to commit to its requirements in action and omission. Intellect (العقل) is used for the discernment of good from evil and the beneficial from the harmful, and sometimes it is used to mean its ultimate purpose, which is to commit to its requirements in seeking good and benefit and avoiding evil and harm. The Exalted said: لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ لا يَفْقَهُونَ بِها وَلَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ لا يُبْصِرُونَ بِها وَلَهُمْ آذانٌ لا يَسْمَعُونَ بِها أُولئِكَ كَالْأَنْعامِ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ — They have hearts with which they do not understand, they have eyes with which they do not see, and they have ears with which they do not hear. They are like cattle; rather they are more astray. (7:179).

Most of those who benefit from hearing are the common people, due to their inability to intellectually grasp the subtleties of matters, perceive their reality, and be guided to their benefits and harms. Only the elite (الخاصة) benefit from the intellect.

Thus, in His saying: لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ (Had we listened or applied reason), hearing is intended as responding to the call of the messengers and committing to the requirements of their speech, for they are the sincere and trustworthy advisors. By intellect is intended committing to the requirements of the truth they call to by intellectually grasping it and being intellectually guided to the fact that it is the truth and that it is mandatory for a human being to submit to the truth.

Hearing was mentioned before intellect because its use is the characteristic of the common people, who are the majority, while intellect is the characteristic of the elite, who are few individuals.

The meaning is: had we in the world obeyed the messengers in their advice and exhortations, or had we intellectually grasped the proof of the truth, we would not be today among the companions of the Blaze, who are the inhabitants of the Fire eternally.

It is said: Hearing and intellect were combined because the basis of religious obligation (التكليف) rests upon the proofs of hearing (revelation) and intellect.

His saying, the Exalted: فَاعْتَرَفُوا بِذَنْبِهِمْ فَسُحْقاً لِأَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ — Thus they will confess their sin. So away with the inmates of the Blaze! (11). They only said: لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ ما كُنَّا فِي أَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ out of regret for what they neglected regarding the side of Allah and the good they missed for themselves. Thus, they confessed that what they committed resulted in entering the Fire and that they should not have committed it; this is the sin (الذنب), and so they confessed their sin.

The word "sin" (الذنب) was used in the singular based on the intention of its root meaning (المصدر), as it is originally a verbal noun.

His saying: فَسُحْقاً لِأَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ (So away with the inmates of the Blaze!). Suhq (السحق) is the crushing of a thing into fragments, as mentioned by Al-Raghib, and it is a supplication against them.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَيْبِ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ — Indeed those who fear their Lord in secret will have forgiveness and a great reward. (12). When He mentioned the state of the disbelievers and what they are recompensed with for their disbelief, He contrasted it with the state of the believers in the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — to complete the division. He mentioned "fear" (الخشية) as their attribute because the context is one of warning and threat.

He described their fear as being "in the Unseen" because that which they believe in is veiled from them behind the veils of the Unseen.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَسِرُّوا قَوْلَكُمْ أَوِ اجْهَرُوا بِهِ إِنَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ — Whether you keep your discourse secret or make it public, He knows well what is in the breasts. (13). This removes a doubt that might occur in their hearts based on a sense of improbability. This is because He, the Exalted, directed the discourse toward explaining His Lordship over everything, which entails the Resurrection and recompense, and He mentioned His absolute kingdom and power, and His creation and governance, but He did not mention His knowledge (علمه) which encompasses them and their states and deeds — and the Resurrection and recompense cannot be completed without it.

It was possible for them to imagine that deeds, in their vast number beyond counting, could not be recorded, especially what the breasts conceal. For man measures things by himself and weighs them by his own scale, and he is unable to count the particulars of deeds, which are various and passing movements, especially the deeds of the hearts hidden in their corners.

So He refuted this by stating that making speech public or hiding it is the same in relation to Him, the Exalted, for He is knowledgeable of what is within the breasts. The context bears witness that what is intended is the equality of the hidden and manifest aspects of deeds in relation to Him. He only mentioned the secrets of speech and its publicity because the meaning of concealment and manifestation is apparent in public and secret speech.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ خَلَقَ وَهُوَ اللَّطِيفُ الْخَبِيرُ — Would He not know those He has created, while He is the All-attentive, the All-aware? (14). This is a rhetorical question (استفهام إنكاري) taken as a proof for His knowledge, the Exalted, of the deeds of creation, their manifest and hidden, their secret and public. This is because the deeds of creation — including the voluntary deeds of man — even if attributed to their agents, Allah the Glorified is the one who wills them and brings them into existence through the path of human choice and the requirement of other causes. He is the Creator of the essences of things and the one who ordains for them their effects, whatever they may be, and the link between them and their effects, bringing them to their effects. The Exalted said: اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَكِيلٌ — Allah is the creator of all things, and He is the guardian of all things (39:62), and He said: الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى وَالَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدى — who created and proportioned, who determined and guided (87:2-3). Thus, He, the Glorified, encompasses the essence of what He created and its effect; and among its effects are its manifest and hidden deeds, what it conceals and what it makes public. How then could He encompass it and not know it?

In the verse is an indication that the states of things and their deeds are not outside of their creation, because He, the Exalted, argued from His knowledge of "whom He created" to His knowledge of the specifics of their states and deeds. Had the states and deeds been outside the existence of their subjects, the argumentation would not be complete.

Furthermore, states and deeds are among the requirements of their subjects, and that to which the existence of a thing is attributed, the effects of its existence are also attributed to it.

His saying: وَهُوَ اللَّطِيفُ الْخَبِيرُ (while He is the All-attentive, the All-aware). That is, the one who penetrates into the inner depths of things, informed of the particulars of their existence and their effects. The sentence is circumstantial (حالية), providing the reason for what preceded it. These two noble names are among the Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) with which the verse was concluded to emphasize its content.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Sufyan ibn 'Uyaynah, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً (that He may test you [to see] which of you is best in conduct). He said: ليس يعني أكثركم عملا ولكن أصوبكم عملا ، وإنما الإصابة خشية الله والنية الصادقة والخشية. — "It does not mean the one of you with the most deeds, but the one of you with the most correct deeds. Correctness is only the fear of Allah, the sincere intention, and godwariness (الخشية)."

Then he said: الإبقاء على العمل حتى يخلص أشد من العمل. ألا والعمل الخالص ـ الذي لا تريد أن يحمدك عليه أحد إلا الله ، والنية أفضل من العمل ألا وإن النية هي العمل. — "Maintaining the deed until it becomes sincere is harder than the deed itself. Behold, the sincere deed is that for which you do not want anyone to praise you except Allah. The intention is better than the deed; behold, the intention is the deed itself." Then he recited His saying: قُلْ كُلٌّ يَعْمَلُ عَلى شاكِلَتِهِ — Say, ‘Everyone acts according to his character’ (17:84), meaning according to his intention.

In al-Majma', Abu Qatadah said: "I asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about His saying, the Exalted: أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً (which of you is best in conduct), what did He mean by it? He said: يقول : أيكم أحسن عقلا. — 'He says: which of you is best in intellect.' Then he said: أتمكم عقلا وأشدكم لله خوفا ، وأحسنكم فيما أمر الله به ونهى عنه نظرا ـ وإن كان أقلكم تطوعا. — 'The most complete of you in intellect, the most intense of you in fear of Allah, and the best of you in observing what Allah has commanded and forbidden — even if he is the least of you in supererogatory acts.'"

In the same source, from Ibn 'Umar, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he recited His saying, the Exalted: تَبارَكَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ الْمُلْكُ until His saying أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً, then he said: أيكم أحسن عقلا ، وأورع عن محارم الله وأسرع في طاعة الله. — "Which of you is best in intellect, most wary of the prohibitions of Allah, and quickest in the obedience of Allah."

In Tafsir al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ طِباقاً (He, who created seven heavens in layers). He said: بعضها طبق لبعض. — "Some of them are a layer for others."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: مِنْ تَفاوُتٍ (any discordance). He said: من فساد. — "Any corruption."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ ارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ (Then look again). He said: انظر في ملكوت السماوات والأرض. — "Look into the kingdom of the heavens and the earth."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: بِمَصابِيحَ (with lamps). He said: بالنجوم. — "With the stars."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: سَمِعُوا لَها شَهِيقاً (they hear it gasping). He said: وقعا. — "A thudding sound."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: تَكادُ تَمَيَّزُ مِنَ الْغَيْظِ (It almost bursts with fury). He said: على أعداء الله. — "Against the enemies of Allah."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ ـ ما كُنَّا فِي أَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ (And they will say, ‘Had we listened or applied reason, we would not have been among the inmates of the Blaze’). He said: قد سمعوا وعقلوا ولكنهم لم يطيعوا ولم يقبلوا ، والدليل على أنهم قد سمعوا وعقلوا ولم يقبلوا ، قوله : « فَاعْتَرَفُوا بِذَنْبِهِمْ فَسُحْقاً لِأَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ » . — "They had indeed heard and reasoned, but they did not obey and did not accept. The proof that they had heard and reasoned but did not accept is His saying: 'Thus they will confess their sin. So away with the inmates of the Blaze!'"

I say: He (peace be upon him) means that the evidence that the lack of hearing and intellect refers to the lack of obedience and acceptance after hearing and reasoning is that He, the Exalted, called their saying an "admission of sin" (اعترافا بالذنب). An act is not considered a sin on the part of its doer except after knowledge of the aspect of its evil through hearing or intellect.

Ayahs

هُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ ذَلُولًا فَٱمْشُوا۟ فِى مَنَاكِبِهَا وَكُلُوا۟ مِن رِّزْقِهِۦ ۖ وَإِلَيْهِ ٱلنُّشُورُ (١٥)
It is He who made the earth tractable for you; so walk on its flanks and eat of His provision, and towards Him is the resurrection. (15)
ءَأَمِنتُم مَّن فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ أَن يَخْسِفَ بِكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ فَإِذَا هِىَ تَمُورُ (١٦)
Are you secure that He who is in the sky will not make the earth swallow you while it quakes? (16)
أَمْ أَمِنتُم مَّن فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ أَن يُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ حَاصِبًا ۖ فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ كَيْفَ نَذِيرِ (١٧)
Are you secure that He who is in the sky will not unleash upon you a rain of stones? Soon you will know how My warning has been! (17)
وَلَقَدْ كَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ نَكِيرِ (١٨)
Certainly those who were before them had impugned [My apostles]; but then how was My rebuttal! (18)
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ إِلَى ٱلطَّيْرِ فَوْقَهُمْ صَـٰٓفَّـٰتٍ وَيَقْبِضْنَ ۚ مَا يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍۭ بَصِيرٌ (١٩)
Have they not regarded the birds above them spreading and closing their wings? No one sustains them except the All-beneficent. Indeed, He watches all things. (19)
أَمَّنْ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى هُوَ جُندٌ لَّكُمْ يَنصُرُكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ۚ إِنِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ إِلَّا فِى غُرُورٍ (٢٠)
Who is it that is your host who may help you, besides the All-beneficent? The faithless only dwell in delusion. (20)
أَمَّنْ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى يَرْزُقُكُمْ إِنْ أَمْسَكَ رِزْقَهُۥ ۚ بَل لَّجُّوا۟ فِى عُتُوٍّ وَنُفُورٍ (٢١)
Who is it that may provide for you if He withholds His provision? Indeed, they persist in defiance and aversion. (21)
أَفَمَن يَمْشِى مُكِبًّا عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهِۦٓ أَهْدَىٰٓ أَمَّن يَمْشِى سَوِيًّا عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٢٢)
Is he who walks prone on his face better guided, or he who walks upright on a straight path? (22)

(Commentary)

The verses proceed, one after another, with signs of divine governance (التدبير) that point toward His Lordship (الربوبية), the Exalted, coupled with warning and intimidation. I refer to His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ ذَلُولاً "It is He who made the earth gentle for you..." (67:15), and His saying: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا إِلَى الطَّيْرِ "Have they not regarded the birds..." (67:19), following His saying: الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْمَوْتَ وَالْحَياةَ "He who created death and life..." (67:2), His saying: الَّذِي خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَماواتٍ "He who created seven heavens..." (67:3), and His saying: وَلَقَدْ زَيَّنَّا "We have certainly adorned..." (67:5).

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ ذَلُولاً فَامْشُوا فِي مَناكِبِها وَكُلُوا مِنْ رِزْقِهِ وَإِلَيْهِ النُّشُورُ "It is He who made the earth gentle for you. So walk its flanks and eat of His provision, and to Him is the resurrection." (67:15). The term "gentle" (الذلول) refers to a mount that is easy to ride, neither agitated nor rebellious. "Flanks" (المناكب) is the plural of mankib, which is the junction between the upper arm and the shoulder; it is used here metaphorically for the surface of the earth. Al-Raghib stated: "Its metaphorical use for the earth is like the metaphorical use of 'back' (الظهر) for it in His saying: ما تَرَكَ عَلى ظَهْرِها مِنْ دَابَّةٍ 'He would not leave any creature on its back' (35:45)." Describing the earth as "gentle" and designating its surfaces as "flanks" upon which one settles and walks is in consideration of its submission to various human activities without resistance. Various explanations have been offered for it being "gentle with flanks," all of which return to what we have mentioned.

The command in His saying, "and eat of His provision," is for permissibility (الإباحة). "Resurrection" (النشور والنشر) refers to the reviving of the dead after their death; its linguistic root is from the spreading out (نشر) of a scroll or a garment when it is unfolded after being folded.

The meaning is: He is the one who made the earth compliant and submissive to you, enabling you to settle upon its back and walk through it, eating from His provision which He has decreed for you through various forms of seeking and labor therein.

His saying, وَإِلَيْهِ النُّشُورُ "and to Him is the resurrection," means that the spreading out of the dead—by bringing them out of the earth and reviving them for reckoning and recompense—returns to Him alone. Restricting the return of the resurrection to Him is an allusion to the fact that the judgment of resurrection and the giving of life on the Day of Resurrection belong exclusively to Him. Thus, He is your Lord, the Governor (المدبر) of the affairs of your worldly life by establishing you upon the earth and guiding you to the requirements of life, and to Him belongs the judgment of resurrection for reckoning and recompense.

In describing the earth as "gentle" and humans being upon its "flanks," there is a clear hint toward what modern scientific research has concluded regarding the earth being a moving sphere.

His saying, the Exalted: أَأَمِنْتُمْ مَنْ فِي السَّماءِ أَنْ يَخْسِفَ بِكُمُ الْأَرْضَ فَإِذا هِيَ تَمُورُ "Are you secure that He who is in the sky will not make the earth swallow you while it quakes?" (67:16). This is a warning and intimidation following the establishment of proof, and a rebuke for their negligence regarding the matter of Lordship (الربوبية) and their failure to show gratitude for the blessings of their Lord by submitting to His Lordship and rejecting the rivals (الأنداد) they have fabricated.

What is meant by "He who is in the sky" are the angels residing therein, who are entrusted with the events of the cosmos. The singular pronoun refers back to "who" (مَنْ) in consideration of its literal form. "Swallowing" (خسف) the earth with a people means splitting it and making them disappear into its belly. "Quaking" (المور), according to al-Majma', is the agitation of moving to and fro, like the movement of waves.

The meaning is: Do you feel secure, in your disbelief in His Lordship, from the angels residing in the sky—who are entrusted with the affairs of the world—that they might split the earth and cause you to disappear within it by Allah's command, such that the earth then agitates to and fro with its earthquake?

It has been said that "He who is in the sky" refers to Allah, the Glorified, and His being "in the sky" refers to His authority, governance, and command being there, as it is impossible for Him, the Exalted, to be in a place (مكان) or direction (جهة), or to be encompassed by any of the worlds. While this meaning is acceptable in itself, it is contrary to the apparent (الظاهر) meaning of the text.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ أَمِنْتُمْ مَنْ فِي السَّماءِ أَنْ يُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ حاصِباً فَستَعْلَمُونَ كَيْفَ نَذِيرِ "Or are you secure that He who is in the sky will not send against you a mushrooming storm? Soon you will know how My warning was!" (67:17). A "mushrooming storm" (الحاصب) is a wind that carries pebbles and stones. The meaning is: Do you feel secure from the one in the sky sending upon you a wind carrying pebbles and stones, just as He sent it upon the people of Lot? He, the Exalted, said: إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنا عَلَيْهِمْ حاصِباً إِلَّا آلَ لُوطٍ "Indeed, We sent a mushrooming storm against them, except the family of Lot" (54:34).

His saying, فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ كَيْفَ نَذِيرِ "Soon you will know how My warning was," uses "warning" (النذير) as a verbal noun meaning the act of warning (الإنذار). The sentence follows from what is understood from the preceding speech regarding their disbelief in His Lordship and their feeling secure from His punishment. The meaning is clear.

It has been said that "the warner" (النذير) is an adjective meaning "the one who warns," referring to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, but this is a weak (سخيف) interpretation.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ كَذَّبَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَكَيْفَ كانَ نَكِيرِ "Certainly those who were before them denied; so how was My rebuttal?" (67:18). What is meant by "rebuttal" (النكير) is the punishment and the alteration of blessing, or the denial (الإنكار). The verse serves as a witness (الشاهد) to the truth of the threat and intimidation found in His saying: "Soon you will know how My warning was."

The meaning is: Certainly those nations who passed before them denied My messengers and rejected My Lordship; so how was My punishment and My alteration of the blessings upon them, or how was My denial of their conduct when I destroyed them and uprooted them?

In this verse, there is a transition (التفات) from direct address to the third person in His saying, "before them" (مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ). This signals that—due to their ignorance and their failure to contemplate the signs of Lordship, and their lack of fear regarding their Lord's wrath—they have fallen from the honor of being directly addressed. Thus, He turned away from addressing them regarding the knowledge being conveyed, directing the speech instead to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا إِلَى الطَّيْرِ فَوْقَهُمْ صافَّاتٍ وَيَقْبِضْنَ ما يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا الرَّحْمنُ إِنَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ بَصِيرٌ "Have they not regarded the birds above them, spreading their wings and folding them? None holds them except the All-beneficent. Indeed, He sees all things." (67:19). The birds being "above them" refers to their flight in the air. "Spreading" (صفيف) refers to the bird extending its wings during flight, and "folding" (قبض) refers to it drawing its wings in. The plural forms in "spreading" (صافَّاتٍ) and "folding" (يَقْبِضْنَ) are used because "the birds" (الطير) refers to the entire genus.

His saying, ما يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا الرَّحْمنُ "None holds them except the All-beneficent," acts as an answer to a hypothetical question, as if a questioner were to ask: "What is the purpose of drawing their attention to the spreading and folding of the birds' wings above them?" The answer is given: "None holds them except the All-beneficent."

The stability of the bird while flying in the air without falling—even if it relies on natural causes (أسباب طبيعية), just as a human's stability on the surface of the earth or a fish's in water relies on natural causes that ultimately trace back to Him, the Exalted—is highlighted because some events do not have an obvious cause to a person at first glance. When one observes such things, it is easy to transition to the realization that Allah, the Glorified, is the Supreme Cause (السبب الأعلى) to whom their occurrence and existence ultimately return. Therefore, Allah, the Glorified, alerted them in His speech by directing their gaze toward these things, using them as evidence for His oneness in Lordship (الربوبية).

Much of this kind exists in His speech, the Exalted, such as the upholding of the heavens without pillars, the upholding of the earth, the preservation of ships upon the water, and the variation of fruits, colors, and languages. In these cases, the immediate natural cause (السبب الطبيعي القريب) is somewhat hidden, making it easy for a simple mind to transition to attributing them to Him, the Exalted. Then, if one becomes aware of the existence of their immediate causes through a type of intellectual exertion, he finds the exact same need for a cause within those causes, until it ends with Him, the Exalted—and indeed, to your Lord is the ultimate end.

The author of al-Kashshaf said: "If you ask: Why was it said 'and they fold' (يَقْبِضْنَ) [using a verb] and not 'and folders' (قابضات) [using a participle]? I say: Because the primary state in flight is the spreading of wings, for flying in the air is like swimming in water. The primary state in swimming is the extension and spreading of limbs. As for folding, it is incidental to the spreading, used to assist in movement. Thus, that which is incidental and not primary was expressed with a verb, meaning they are 'spreaders' (صافات) and 'folding' occurs from them occasionally, just as it does with a swimmer." [End of quote].

This is based on the verse being the totality of His saying, "spreading and folding," which constitutes flight. However, it is also possible to infer that one sign (الآية) is their not falling while they are spreading their wings, and another sign is that they sometimes fold them and yet do not fall even while folding.

The elegance (اللطف) in mentioning the flight of birds in the air after mentioning the making of the earth gentle and man being upon its flanks is not hidden.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمَّنْ هذَا الَّذِي هُوَ جُنْدٌ لَكُمْ يَنْصُرُكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ الرَّحْمنِ إِنِ الْكافِرُونَ إِلَّا فِي غُرُورٍ "Who is it that will be a host for you to help you besides the All-beneficent? The faithless are only in delusion." (67:20). This is a rebuke and a reprimand for their taking gods besides Allah to help them. For this reason, the speech transitions from the third person to direct address, to make the reprimand more severe upon them.

His saying, أَمَّنْ هذَا الَّذِي etc., means: "Rather, who is this who can be pointed to and said: 'This is a host for you who will help you against the All-beneficent if He intends you harm or punishment?'" There is none besides Allah who can help you against Him. This points to their error in taking some of Allah's creation as gods to help them in calamities, while they are owned by Allah, possessing neither benefit nor harm for themselves, let alone for others.

Since they have no answer, He, the Exalted, answers by saying: إِنِ الْكافِرُونَ إِلَّا فِي غُرُورٍ "The faithless are only in delusion," meaning that delusion (الغرور) has encompassed and overwhelmed them, making their claims about the divinity of their gods seem real to them.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمَّنْ هذَا الَّذِي يَرْزُقُكُمْ إِنْ أَمْسَكَ رِزْقَهُ بَلْ لَجُّوا فِي عُتُوٍّ وَنُفُورٍ "Who is it that will provide for you if He withholds His provision? Rather, they persist in rebellion and aversion." (67:21). That is: "Rather, who is this who can be pointed to as the one who will provide for you if Allah withholds His provision, taking His place in providing for you?" Then He, the Glorified, answered by saying: بَلْ لَجُّوا فِي عُتُوٍّ وَنُفُورٍ "Rather, they persist in rebellion and aversion," meaning that the truth has become clear to them, yet they do not submit to the truth by believing in it and following it. Instead, they have persisted in their distance from the truth and their aversion to it, and they have been stubborn (لجوا) in that.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَمَنْ يَمْشِي مُكِبًّا عَلى وَجْهِهِ أَهْدى أَمَّنْ يَمْشِي سَوِيًّا عَلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ "Is he who walks prone on his face better guided, or he who walks upright on a straight path?" (67:22). To "fall prone" (إكباب) on one's face means to fall down upon it. The author of al-Kashshaf said: "The meaning of akabba is to enter into a state of falling and to become prone."

This is a rhetorical question (استفهام إنكاري) regarding the equality of the two states, serving as an indirect reference (تعريض) to them after the veil of the third person was drawn over them, depriving them of the honor of presence and direct address once their stubbornness had become settled. The meaning is that, in their stubbornness, they are in a strange state of rebellion and aversion to the truth, like one who travels a path while falling prone on his face, unable to see the elevations, depressions, slippery spots, or obstacles in the road. Such a traveler is not like one who walks upright on a straight path (صراط مستقيم), seeing where he places his feet and the path ahead of him with clarity, as well as the goal he intends.

These disbelievers are traveling the path of life while they oppose the truth despite knowing it; they turn a blind eye to the knowledge they ought to recognize and the actions they ought to perform. They do not submit to the truth so that they might have insight (بصيرة) into the matter and travel the path of life while upright on a straight path, thereby being safe from destruction.

It has become clear that what is in the verse is a general parable (مثل عام) representing the state of the ignorant, stubborn disbeliever who persists in his ignorance, and the insightful believer who seeks the truth.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Sa'd, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "Hearts are of four types: a heart in which there is hypocrisy and faith, a heart that is inverted (منكوس), a heart that is sealed (مطبوع), and a heart that is radiant (أزهر)." I asked: "What is the radiant one?" He said: "It contains something like the likeness of a lamp.

As for the sealed heart, it is the heart of the hypocrite. As for the radiant heart, it is the heart of the believer—if He gives to him, he is grateful, and if He tries him, he is patient. As for the inverted heart, it is the heart of the polytheist." Then he recited this verse: أَفَمَنْ يَمْشِي مُكِبًّا عَلى وَجْهِهِ أَهْدى أَمَّنْ يَمْشِي سَوِيًّا عَلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ "Is he who walks prone on his face better guided, or he who walks upright on a straight path?" (67:22). "As for the heart in which there is faith and hypocrisy, they were a people in al-Ta'if—if the end of one of them reached him while in his hypocrisy, he perished, and if it reached him while in his faith, he was saved."

I say: It was also narrated in Tafsir al-Burhan from Ibn Babawayh, with his chain of transmission from al-Fudayl, from Sa'd al-Khaffaf, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, hearts are of four types..." and he recounted the hadith to its end, except that it contains: "and a radiant, luminous heart."

His saying: "they were a people in al-Ta'if," refers to the satanic visitation (الطائف الشيطاني) that sometimes touches a person. The Exalted said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا إِذا مَسَّهُمْ طائِفٌ مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ تَذَكَّرُوا فَإِذا هُمْ مُبْصِرُونَ "When those who are God-conscious are touched by a visitation from Satan, they remember [Allah] and then they have insight" (7:201). The meaning is that they live with a satanic visitation that touches them from time to time; if their end reaches them while the visitation is with them, they perish, and if it reaches them while they are in a state of faith, they are saved.

Know that there are narrations that apply His saying: أَفَمَنْ يَمْشِي مُكِبًّا عَلى وَجْهِهِ "Is he who walks prone on his face..." to those who deviate from the guardianship (ولاية) of Ali (peace be upon him) and those who follow and love him. These are instances of interpretive application (الجري), and Allah knows best.

Ayahs

قُلْ هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنشَأَكُمْ وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ وَٱلْأَفْـِٔدَةَ ۖ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ (٢٣)
Say, ‘It is He who created you, and made for you hearing, eyesight, and hearts. Little do you thank.’ (23)
قُلْ هُوَ ٱلَّذِى ذَرَأَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَإِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (٢٤)
Say, ‘It is He who created you on the earth, and toward Him you will be mustered.’ (24)
وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٢٥)
They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’ (25)
قُلْ إِنَّمَا ٱلْعِلْمُ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَإِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٢٦)
Say, ‘Its knowledge is only with Allah; I am only a manifest warner.’ (26)
فَلَمَّا رَأَوْهُ زُلْفَةً سِيٓـَٔتْ وُجُوهُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَقِيلَ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى كُنتُم بِهِۦ تَدَّعُونَ (٢٧)
When they see it brought near, the countenances of the faithless will be contorted, and [they will be] told, ‘This is what you were asking for!’ (27)
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَهْلَكَنِىَ ٱللَّهُ وَمَن مَّعِىَ أَوْ رَحِمَنَا فَمَن يُجِيرُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ مِنْ عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٢٨)
Say, ‘Tell me, whether Allah destroys me and those who are with me, or He has mercy on us, who will shelter the faithless from a painful punishment?’ (28)
قُلْ هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ءَامَنَّا بِهِۦ وَعَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْنَا ۖ فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ هُوَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٢٩)
Say, ‘He is the All-beneficent; we have faith in Him, and in Him do we trust. Soon you will know who is in manifest error.’ (29)
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَصْبَحَ مَآؤُكُمْ غَوْرًا فَمَن يَأْتِيكُم بِمَآءٍ مَّعِينٍۭ (٣٠)
Say, ‘Tell me, should your water sink down [into the ground], who will bring you running water?’ (30)

(Commentary)

These are other verses through which Allah, the Exalted, reminds them, indicating His oneness in creation (الخلق) and governance (التدبير) — the divine management and regulation of the affairs of the universe — coupled with warning (الإنذار) and intimidation (التخويف). They follow the primary purpose of the Surah, which is to remind of Divine Oneness (الوحدانية) alongside the warning. However, since the Exalted had pointed to their persistence and stubbornness against the truth in His previous saying: بَل لَّجُّوا فِي عُتُوٍّ وَنُفُورٍ "Rather they persist in rebellion and aversion," He changed the discourse by turning away from addressing them directly and turning toward addressing the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم, commanding him to confront them and strike their ears with His signs in creation and governance that indicate His uniqueness in Lordship (الربوبية), and to warn them of Allah’s punishment. This is found in His sayings: قُلْ هُوَ الَّذِي أَنشَأَكُمْ "Say, ‘It is He who created you...’", قُلْ هُوَ الَّذِي ذَرَأَكُمْ "Say, ‘It is He who scattered you...’", قُلْ إِنَّمَا الْعِلْمُ "Say, ‘The knowledge is only...’", قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَهْلَكَنِيَ اللَّهُ "Say, ‘Tell me, if Allah were to destroy me...’", قُلْ هُوَ الرَّحْمَنُ "Say, ‘He is the All-beneficent...’", and قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَصْبَحَ مَاؤُكُمْ غَوْرًا "Say, ‘Tell me, if your water were to disappear...’"

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ هُوَ الَّذِي أَنشَأَكُمْ وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصَارَ وَالْأَفْئِدَةَ ۖ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ Say, ‘It is He who created you, and made for you hearing, sight, and hearts. Little is the thanks you offer.’ Origination (الإنشاء) is to bring a thing into existence initially and to nurture it.

The tone of reproach in the tail of the verse in His saying: قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ "Little is the thanks you offer"—the like of which is repeated in several places of His speech, such as in Surah al-Mu’minun (1) and Alif Lam Mim al-Sajdah (2)—indicates that the Exalted’s origination of man and His equipping him with the apparatus of sensation (الحس) and thought (الفكر) are among His greatest favors, the value of which cannot be fully measured.

The meaning of His origination (إنشائه) is not merely His creating him in any manner whatsoever, but rather His creating and bringing him forth without a prior precedent in his matter (المادة), as He pointed out when describing his creation stage by stage: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنسَانَ مِن سُلَالَةٍ مِّن طِينٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَاهُ نُطْفَةً فِي قَرَارٍ مَّكِينٍ ثُمَّ خَلَقْنَا النُّطْفَةَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقْنَا الْعَلَقَةَ مُضْغَةً "Certainly We created man from an extract of clay. Then We made him a drop of [seminal] fluid [lodged] in a secure abode. Then We created the drop of fluid as a clinging mass. Then We created the clinging mass as a fleshy tissue..." until He said: ثُمَّ أَنشَأْنَاهُ خَلْقًا آخَرَ "...then We produced him as another creation" (al-Mu’minun: 14). Thus, the transformation of the fleshy tissue (المضغة) into a hearing, seeing, and thinking human being—by the composition of the human soul (النفس) upon it—is "another creation" (خلق آخر) that does not resemble the types of material creation (الخلقة المادية) that occur to the matter of man, such as taking it from the earth, then making it a drop of fluid, then a clinging mass, then a fleshy tissue. These are merely successive material stages, whereas his becoming a conscious human being has no prior precedent that is identical or similar to it; hence, it is "origination" (الإنشاء).

Similar to this is His saying: وَمِنْ آيَاتِهِ أَنْ خَلَقَكُم مِّن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ إِذَا أَنتُم بَشَرٌ تَنتَشِرُونَ Of His signs is that He created you from dust, then, behold, you are humans scattering [everywhere]! (al-Rum: 20). (Note the position of the "suddenness" particle (إِذَا الفجائية)).

So His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي أَنشَأَكُمْ "It is He who created you" is an allusion to the creation of man.

And His saying: وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصَارَ وَالْأَفْئِدَةَ "and made for you hearing, sight, and hearts" is an allusion to equipping him with the apparatus of sensation and thought. This "making" (الجعل) is an originative act (إنشائي), just like the making of the human soul itself, as indicated by His saying: وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَنشَأَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصَارَ وَالْأَفْئِدَةَ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ It is He who made for you hearing, sight, and hearts. Little is the thanks you offer. (al-Mu’minun: 78).

Thus, man—by the specificity of his origination and his being such that he hears and sees—is distinguished from minerals and plants. The restriction to hearing and sight among the other senses, like touch, taste, and smell, is because they are the primary ones—and it is not unlikely that "hearing and sight" refer to all external senses (الحواس الظاهرة) by way of mentioning the part and intending the whole. And by the "heart" (الفؤاد), which is the thinking soul (النفس المتفكرة), he is distinguished from all other animals.

(1) Verse 78. (2) Verse 9.

And His saying: قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ "Little is the thanks you offer" means you give little thanks for this great favor—or favors. The particle (ما) is extra (زائدة), and "little" (قليلاً) is a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق), the estimation of which is: "you thank with a little thanking." It has also been said that (ما) is infinitive (مصدرية), meaning: "little is your thanking."

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ هُوَ الَّذِي ذَرَأَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَإِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ Say, ‘It is He who scattered you in the earth, and to Him you will be mustered.’ Scattering (الذرء) is creation (الخلق). The meaning of scattering them in the earth is creating them attached to the earth, such that their perfection is not completed except through actions related to earthly matter, which Allah, the Exalted, has adorned with that which attracts the human soul in its immediate life, so that the righteous may be distinguished from the wicked. The Exalted said: إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا مَا عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَّهَا لِنَبْلُوَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا وَإِنَّا لَجَاعِلُونَ مَا عَلَيْهَا صَعِيدًا جُرُزًا "Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it that We may test them [to see] which of them is best in conduct. And indeed We will turn whatever is on it into a barren plain." (al-Kahf: 7-8).

And His saying: وَإِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ "and to Him you will be mustered" is an allusion to the Resurrection (البعث) and Recompense (الجزاء), and it is a decisive promise.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَٰذَا الْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’ What is meant by "this promise" is the promised Mustering (الحشر), and this is a request for haste on their part out of mockery.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنَّمَا الْعِلْمُ عِندَ اللَّهِ وَإِنَّمَا أَنَا نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ Say, ‘The knowledge is only with Allah, and I am only a manifest warner.’ This is a response to their saying: "When will this promise be fulfilled...". Its summary is that the knowledge of it is with Allah; none knows it but He, as He said: لَا يُجَلِّيهَا لِوَقْتِهَا إِلَّا هُوَ "None shall manifest it at its time but He" (al-A'raf: 187). I am nothing but a manifest warner (نذير مبين) commanded to inform you that you will be mustered to Him; as for when it will be, I have no knowledge of that.

This is based on what is suggested by the verse's occurrence in the context of answering the question about the time of the Mustering. Accordingly, the definite article (اللام) in "the knowledge" (العلم) would be for mental reference (للعهد), meaning the knowledge of the time of the Mustering. However, if it were for the genus (للجنس)—as the sentence إِنَّمَا الْعِلْمُ عِندَ اللَّهِ "The knowledge is only with Allah" suggests in itself—then the meaning would be: "The reality of knowledge (حقيقة العلم) is only with Allah, and nothing of it can be encompassed except by His permission," as He said: وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَيْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِ إِلَّا بِمَا شَاءَ "and they do not comprehend anything of His knowledge except what He wishes" (al-Baqarah: 255). He did not wish for me to know of that except that it will occur, and I warn you of it; as for when it will occur, I have no knowledge of it.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا رَأَوْهُ زُلْفَةً سِيئَتْ وُوجُوهُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا When they see it near at hand, the faces of the faithless will be distorted... Proximity (الزلفة) means nearness (القرب). What is meant by it is "near" (القريب), or it is used as an intensive noun. The pronoun in رَأَوْهُ "they see it" refers to the promise, and it is said it refers to the punishment. The meaning is:

When they see the aforementioned promise near, having loomed over them, that will grieve the faces of those who disbelieved in it, and the traces of disappointment and loss will appear in their features (سيماهم).

And His saying: وَقِيلَ هَٰذَا الَّذِي كُنتُم بِهِ تَدَّعُونَ ...and it will be said, ‘This is what you used to call for!’ It is said that (تَدَّعُونَ) and (تَدَّعُونَ) [with different vocalizations] carry the same meaning, like (تَدَّخِرُونَ) and (تَدَّخِرُونَ). The meaning is: It will be said to them: "This is the promise that you used to ask for and seek to hasten by your saying: 'When will this promise be fulfilled?'" The apparent context suggests that the speakers are the angels by Allah's command, though it is also said that the speakers are the disbelievers saying it to one another.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَهْلَكَنِيَ اللَّهُ وَمَن مَّعِيَ أَوْ رَحِمَنَا فَمَن يُجِيرُ الْكَافِرِينَ مِنْ عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ Say, ‘Tell me, if Allah were to destroy me and those who are with me, or have mercy on us, who will shelter the faithless from a painful punishment?’ The particle (إِنْ) is conditional (شرطية); its condition is His saying: "if Allah were to destroy me," and its apodosis (جزاؤها) is His saying: "who will shelter...". The meaning is: Say to them: "Inform me, if Allah were to destroy me and those with me of the believers, or have mercy on us and thus not destroy us, then who is it that will shelter and protect the disbelievers—and you are they, having disbelieved in Allah and thus deserved the painful punishment—from a painful punishment?" He threatens them with a definitive threat, meaning: "My destruction and those with me, or our remaining by the mercy of my Lord, will not benefit you at all regarding the punishment that will certainly afflict you due to your disbelief in Allah."

It is said that the disbelievers of Mecca used to pray for the destruction of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم and the believers. So he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم was commanded to say to them: "If Allah, the Exalted, destroys us or lets us remain, our affair is with Allah and we hope for good from His mercy; but as for you, what will you do? Who will shelter you from a painful punishment for your disbelief in Allah?"

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ هُوَ الرَّحْمَنُ آمَنَّا بِهِ وَعَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْنَا ۖ فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ هُوَ فِي ضَلَالٍ مُّبِينٍ Say, ‘He is the All-beneficent; we have faith in Him, and in Him do we trust. Soon you will know who is in manifest error.’ The pronoun refers to the One Whom he calls to unify, while they pray against him. The meaning is: Say: "The One to Whose Oneness I call you, and Whom you call upon against me and those with me, is the All-Beneficent (الرحمن) Whose favor encompasses everything. We have believed in Him and in Him we have placed our trust (توكلنا), without inclining toward or relying upon anything besides Him. So you will soon know, O disbelievers, who is in manifest error (ضلال مبين)? We or you?"

The author of al-Kashshaf said: "If it is asked: Why was the object of 'we have faith' (آمَنَّا) placed after it, while the object of 'we trust' (تَوَكَّلْنَا) was placed before it? I say: Because the occurrence of 'we have faith' serves as an allusion to the disbelievers when it followed the mention of them, as if it were said: 'We have believed and have not disbelieved as you have disbelieved.' Then he said: 'and in Him [specifically] do we trust,' meaning we do not rely upon what you rely upon of your men and your wealth."

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَصْبَحَ مَاؤُكُمْ غَوْرًا فَمَن يَأْتِيكُم بِمَاءٍ مَّعِينٍ Say, ‘Tell me, if your water were to disappear into the ground, who will bring you running water?’ Sinking (الغور) is the departure of water and its drying up into the earth; what is meant is "sunken" (الغائر). Running water (المعين) is the visible, flowing water. The meaning is: "Inform me, if your water becomes sunken and dried up in the earth, who will bring you visible, flowing water?"

There are narrations that apply these verses to the Mastership (الولاية) of Ali (peace be upon him) and those who oppose him; these are by way of conceptual flow (الجري) and are not explanatory (مفسرة) of the literal meaning.
